quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Mass-energy equivalence"?

options.text: ΣΔV = 0 around any closed loop; based on conservation of energy; EMF gains = resistive drops
Reversible: idealized, infinitely slow, maximum work; irreversible: real, finite speed, always produces less work and more entropy
P = εσAT⁴; total power radiated by a body; P ∝ T⁴ means doubling temperature increases radiation 16-fold
E = mc²; mass is a form of energy; even tiny mass changes correspond to enormous energy due to c² ≈ 9 × 10¹⁶ m²/s²

quiz_questions.explanation: "Mass-energy equivalence" is defined as: E = mc²; mass is a form of energy; even tiny mass changes correspond to enormous energy due to c² ≈ 9 × 10¹⁶ m²/s². The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Atomic mass unit"?

options.text: 1 u = 1.661 × 10⁻²⁷ kg = 931.5 MeV/c²; convenient unit for nuclear mass comparisons
ᴬ_Z X → ᴬ_(Z-1) Y + e⁺ + ν; proton → neutron + positron + neutrino; occurs in proton-rich nuclei
Dark lines on a continuous bright background; produced when cool gas absorbs specific wavelengths from white light
Δm = (Z·m_p + N·m_n) - m_nucleus; the 'missing mass' converted to binding energy; Δm > 0 for all stable nuclei

quiz_questions.explanation: "Atomic mass unit" is defined as: 1 u = 1.661 × 10⁻²⁷ kg = 931.5 MeV/c²; convenient unit for nuclear mass comparisons. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Q-value"?

options.text: The image from lens 1 serves as the object for lens 2; d_o2 = separation - d_i1; apply thin lens equation sequentially
Reversible: idealized, infinitely slow, maximum work; irreversible: real, finite speed, always produces less work and more entropy
θ_incident = θ_reflected; both angles measured from the normal to the surface
Q = (Σm_reactants - Σm_products)c²; Q > 0: exothermic (releases energy); Q < 0: endothermic (requires energy input)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Q-value" is defined as: Q = (Σm_reactants - Σm_products)c²; Q > 0: exothermic (releases energy); Q < 0: endothermic (requires energy input). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Pair production"?

options.text: Central maximum is widest; minima at a sin θ = mλ (m ≠ 0); a = slit width; narrower slit → wider pattern
A gamma ray (E ≥ 1.022 MeV) creates an electron-positron pair near a nucleus: energy → mass; the reverse of annihilation
V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁; ideal transformers conserve power: V₁I₁ = V₂I₂; stepping up voltage steps down current
Dark lines on a continuous bright background; produced when cool gas absorbs specific wavelengths from white light

quiz_questions.explanation: "Pair production" is defined as: A gamma ray (E ≥ 1.022 MeV) creates an electron-positron pair near a nucleus: energy → mass; the reverse of annihilation. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Annihilation"?

options.text: Crossed E and B fields: only particles with v = E/B pass straight through; magnetic and electric forces cancel exactly
ΣΔV = 0 around any closed loop; based on conservation of energy; EMF gains = resistive drops
Each element has a unique set of spectral lines; used for identification from streetlights to distant stars
Particle + antiparticle → 2 gamma rays; mass → energy; e⁻ + e⁺ → 2γ (each 0.511 MeV); complete mass-to-energy conversion

quiz_questions.explanation: "Annihilation" is defined as: Particle + antiparticle → 2 gamma rays; mass → energy; e⁻ + e⁺ → 2γ (each 0.511 MeV); complete mass-to-energy conversion. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "E = mc²; mass is a form of energy; even tiny mass changes correspond to enormous energy due to c² ≈ 9 × 10¹⁶ m²/s²"?

options.text: Self-inductance
Reversible vs. irreversible
Photon absorption
Mass-energy equivalence

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Mass-energy equivalence".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "1 u = 1.661 × 10⁻²⁷ kg = 931.5 MeV/c²; convenient unit for nuclear mass comparisons"?

options.text: Rayleigh criterion
Dispersion
Atomic mass unit
Reversible vs. irreversible

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Atomic mass unit".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Q = (Σm_reactants - Σm_products)c²; Q > 0: exothermic (releases energy); Q < 0: endothermic (requires energy input)"?

options.text: Nuclear binding energy
Root-mean-square speed
Q-value
Electric current

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Q-value".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A gamma ray (E ≥ 1.022 MeV) creates an electron-positron pair near a nucleus: energy → mass; the reverse of annihilation"?

options.text: Heisenberg uncertainty principle
Pair production
Average power
Apparent depth

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Pair production".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Particle + antiparticle → 2 gamma rays; mass → energy; e⁻ + e⁺ → 2γ (each 0.511 MeV); complete mass-to-energy conversion"?

options.text: E-V relationship
Second Law of Thermodynamics
Annihilation
Right-hand rule (force)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Annihilation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Energy and Mass Equivalence is accurate?

options.text: Diverging lenses always produce virtual, upright, reduced images.
Beta-minus decay (β⁻): neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino (Z→Z+1).
Energy and Mass Equivalence is summarized by Einstein's famous equation E = mc².
Photons and Quantum Mechanics introduces the quantized nature of light and energy.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Mass Equivalence as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Energy and Mass Equivalence?

options.text: Lenses use refraction to form images.
Magnetic Fields and Forces explores the interaction between magnetic fields and charged particles.
Beta-minus decay (β⁻): neutron → proton + electron + antineutrino (Z→Z+1).
Mass and energy are interconvertible: the mass defect in nuclear reactions is converted to kinetic energy of products and radiation.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Mass Equivalence as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Energy and Mass Equivalence?

options.text: Brewster's angle: reflected light is completely polarized when tan θ_B = n₂/n₁.
The dielectric reduces the electric field between plates and allows more charge to be stored at the same voltage.
Ideal Gas Behavior is described by the ideal gas law: PV = nRT (or PV = NkT), relating pressure P, volume V, temperature T, and amount of gas n (moles) or N (molecules).
One atomic mass unit (u) = 931.5 MeV/c².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Mass Equivalence as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Energy and Mass Equivalence?

options.text: Lenses use refraction to form images.
Light as a Wave examines the wave nature of light in detail.
The Q-value of a reaction: Q = (Δm)c² = (mass of reactants - mass of products)c²; Q > 0 for exothermic (energy-releasing) reactions.
Magnetic Properties of Materials classifies materials by their response to external magnetic fields.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Mass Equivalence as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Energy and Mass Equivalence?

options.text: The strong nuclear force binds nucleons together, overcoming electrostatic repulsion between protons.
Wave Properties and Behavior explores the fundamental characteristics common to all waves.
Magnetic Fields and Forces explores the interaction between magnetic fields and charged particles.
This equivalence explains why the Sun loses 4 million tons of mass per second as it fuses hydrogen into helium, converting mass to radiant energy.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Energy and Mass Equivalence as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Energy and Mass Equivalence?

options.text: Doppler radar: police speed guns and weather radar measure the frequency shift of reflected radio waves to determine object velocity
The Sun converts 600 million tons of hydrogen into 596 million tons of helium each second; the 4 million ton mass deficit becomes 3.8 × 10²⁶ W of radiant power via E = mc²
Velocity selectors in mass spectrometers: only ions with the correct speed enter the separation chamber, improving measurement accuracy
MRI contrast agents: paramagnetic gadolinium compounds enhance MRI images by altering the magnetic relaxation times of nearby water molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Mass Equivalence.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Energy and Mass Equivalence?

options.text: Laser systems: multi-lens arrangements expand, collimate, and focus laser beams with precise control for applications from surgery to manufacturing
Lightning: charge separation in thunderclouds creates enormous electric fields (≈3 × 10⁶ V/m) that ionize air and create conductive channels, equalizing charge via massive current discharge
PET scans detect positron annihilation: the 1.022 MeV of mass-energy from an electron-positron pair converts to two 0.511 MeV gamma rays traveling in opposite directions
Radio telescope arrays (like the VLA) combine signals from many dishes using interference principles to achieve resolution equivalent to a single dish spanning kilometers

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Mass Equivalence.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Energy and Mass Equivalence applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Radon testing: radon-222 (α emitter, t₁/₂ = 3.8 days) seeps from soil into basements; second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking
Nuclear weapons: a 20-kiloton bomb converts only about 1 gram of matter to energy (E = mc² = 0.001 kg × 9 × 10¹⁶ = 9 × 10¹³ J ≈ 20 kilotons of TNT)
Atomic clocks use the precise frequency of electron transitions in cesium atoms (9.19 GHz) to define the second—accurate to 1 part in 10¹⁴
Car side mirrors are convex ('objects in mirror are closer than they appear') to provide a wider field of view, reducing blind spots

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Mass Equivalence.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Energy and Mass Equivalence?

options.text: The electrical grid: power plants generate at ~20 kV, transformers step up to 110-765 kV for long-distance transmission, then step down to 120/240V for homes—reducing losses by factors of thousands
The Sun converts 600 million tons of hydrogen into 596 million tons of helium each second; the 4 million ton mass deficit becomes 3.8 × 10²⁶ W of radiant power via E = mc²
Gas identification: each gas produces unique spectral lines—used to identify unknown gases in chemistry labs, environmental monitoring, and forensics
Van de Graaff generators accumulate charge on a conducting sphere, creating potentials exceeding 100,000 V—used in nuclear physics and dramatic classroom demonstrations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Mass Equivalence.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Energy and Mass Equivalence in practice?

options.text: Charged particle therapy: proton beams for cancer treatment are steered using electromagnetic forces to precisely target tumors deep in the body
Arrow of time: entropy increase explains why ice melts in warm rooms but warm rooms don't spontaneously freeze—the second law governs the direction of all natural processes
Quantum computing uses superposition and entanglement to process information exponentially faster for certain problems (factoring, simulation, optimization)
PET scans detect positron annihilation: the 1.022 MeV of mass-energy from an electron-positron pair converts to two 0.511 MeV gamma rays traveling in opposite directions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Energy and Mass Equivalence.
